records.ety: [F. dsirer, L. desiderare, origin uncertain, perh. fr. de- + sidus star, constellation, and hence orig., to turn the eyes from the stars. Cf. Consider, and Desiderate, and see Sidereal.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Desired (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Desiring.]
usage: -- To Desire, Wish. In desire the feeling is usually more eager than in wish. “I wish you to do this” is a milder form of command than “I desire you to do this,” though the feeling prompting the injunction may be the same. C. J. Smith.

defs: To long for; to wish for earnestly; to covet.
quotes: Neither shall any man desire thy land.
Ye desire your child to live.

defs: To express a wish for; to entreat; to request.
quotes: Then she said, Did I desire a son of my lord?
Desire him to go in; trouble him no more.

defs: To require; to demand; to claim.
quotes: A doleful case desires a doleful song.
She shall be pleasant while she lives, and desired when she dies.

records.ety: [F. dsir, fr. dsirer. See Desire, v. t.]

defs: The natural longing that is excited by the enjoyment or the thought of any good, and impels to action or effort its continuance or possession; an eager wish to obtain or enjoy.
quotes: Unspeakable desire to see and know.

defs: An expressed wish; a request; petition.
quotes: And slowly was my mother brought To yield consent to my desire.

defs: Anything which is desired; an object of longing.
quotes: The Desire of all nations shall come.

defs: Excessive or morbid longing; lust; appetite.
